turns.content: I just got back from Darwin a few days ago. Had the ranger accreditation workshop, February 20 it started. Three days. I am proper tired.
Three days of workshops can really take it out of you, especially with the travel on top. How did it go?

It was fine. The usual things. Policy updates, first aid refresher, new reporting templates. Some of it is useful. Some of it is the same information we get every time just in a different format. But you have to do it to keep your accreditation current.
Yeah, mandatory renewals tend to be like that — a mix of genuinely useful updates and things you already know. At least it's done for another cycle. Was Darwin manageable?

Darwin is always too much for me. Too many people, too much noise. I stayed with a work colleague near the uni. Could not wait to get back to country. The hardest part was sorting out the kids while I was away.
That's always the tricky part when you have to travel for work. Did you manage to find someone to look after them?

My cousin Yalmay usually looks after Tali and Bunjil when I need to go away. But she was in ceremony business that week so she could not do it. I had to ask around. My neighbour Margaret ended up helping. She is a good woman but she does not know the kids' routines the way Yalmay does.
It's good you had Margaret as a backup, even if it's not quite the same. Did the kids manage alright with her?

Bunjil was fine. He is easy. Tali was a bit difficult apparently. She is 14 and she has her own ideas about everything. Margaret said she would not go to bed on time. But they survived. I rang them every night.
That sounds about right for 14. At least Bunjil was easy and everyone made it through. Calling every night would have helped them feel connected even with you away.
Three days is a long time to be away from them. I do not like it. But the accreditation is not optional. If I do not do the workshop I cannot keep working as a ranger. So you find a way.
It's one of those non-negotiable things. Is it an annual renewal or less frequent?

Every two years for the full workshop. There are smaller online modules through the year but the big one you have to go in person. Next time I will try to make sure Yalmay is available. Or maybe Tali will be old enough to hold things together herself by then.
In two years Tali will be 16, so she might well be able to manage Bunjil for a few days. That would take a lot of the stress out of it for you.
That is what I am hoping. She is growing up fast. Anyway I am just glad to be home. The workshop is done, the paperwork is filed. Back to the real work now, out on country where it makes sense.
Sounds like a relief to have it behind you. Welcome back to country.
